roles.narrative:
Back in college, you lived in a big house with 4 other people, a tight social group that lived and died together. You made a pact at graduation to take a trip together once your lives had gotten settled. Now five years have passed, and that trip still hasn't happened! Of course you’re all busy, but another challenge is finding a plan that feels exciting to everyone.

Although you’ve stayed in touch and visit regularly, you can feel yourselves drifting apart, since you ended up in different cities after graduating and have since formed new social circles. You worry that it’s now or never: if you don’t take this trip soon, you don’t know when you’ll ever find the time. This trip is your chance to reconnect on a deep level, but will only happen if you can find a suitable plan.

**The plan so far:**

Broadly speaking, you share a lot of interests. This is why you’re such great friends. All of you love exploring, and none of you has really traveled outside the USA. You all like cities with nightlife, but you also want the option to dip into nature when you feel like it. That mix is what you’ve been looking for. The solution? Barcelona. You’ve been talking for years about a visit to Barcelona, a culture-rich city that stays up late, with a mountain range on one side and the coast and long beaches on the other. It’s the kind of place that sparked the pact in the first place.

**Topics under discussion:**

However, the devil is in the details. Although you have a lot in common, you also are very different. You became roommates first by random lottery, and then became friends later. For example, you’ve always liked to start your day slow: a coffee, a little quiet, easing into the morning. Some of your friends are the opposite and want to jump into action early.

But life is all about tradeoffs, right? You have to give a little to get a little, and you are confident that you can find a plan that works for everyone.

*As you prepare for the negotiation, you reflect on your general preferences for each topic under discussion and the two issues associated with each one, as summarized below.*

*Topic 1: Travel Pace.*
- [1] Slow mornings: Relaxation is your favorite part of a trip. You prefer slow mornings because you like easing into the day without a clock ruining your mood.
- [2] Multiple afternoon activities: And so, by the afternoon, you want breathing room. Too many activities turns the day into a checklist.

*Topic 2: Who We Connect With.*
- [3] Plans to meet new people: Meeting new people sounds fun. The best part is that those shared moments with new people turn into the kind of stories that always come up later when the group’s reminiscing.
- [4] Other college friends join: However, meeting your other college friends sounds like a chore. You didn’t really like them that much, and you’d honestly rather spend time with strangers than with them.

*Topic 3: Togetherness.*
- [5] Shared rooms: Sharing rooms just feels right to you. You like the late-night chats and the small moments, and waking up in the same place is how the weekend actually feels like time together.
- [6] Some solo activities: You also don’t want solo activities explicitly planned. The whole purpose of this trip is to spend more time together.

*Topic 4: Trip Scope.*
- [7] Visits to nearby towns: Ideally, you’d love to see some of the nearby towns. Visiting somewhere like Tarragona, or seeing the villages surrounding the mountain range feels much more exciting than only staying in the city.
- [8] Hotel arrangements in the city: That’s why you’d prefer being closer to the mountains rather than the city. It’s calmer, and it’s closer to the natural park, and to the other towns you could visit.

*Topic 5: Travel Experience.*
- [9] Long nature excursion: You’re flexible on a long nature excursion. If the group is excited you can go along, and if not, that’s fine too.
- [10] More historical and cultural tours: It’s the same for the history tours. You don’t hate having more of them, but they’re not something you need to make this trip feel special.

**Your Prolific Bonus ($):**

- Your preferences are also captured in a points table shown below.
- Higher points mean you prefer that option more.
- If your group reaches an agreement, the **total points** from the chosen options determine the **bonus** payment you receive from Prolific.
- However, this must be an **official agreement** that everyone votes to accept in the system, which means it must have a **positive score for everyone**. Agreements made verbally will not count.
- If a player is **disconnected** or missing, then unfortunately this is outside of our control and your group cannot reach agreement. In that case, the **best option** is usually to press the **Quit** button and receive the base payment.
- **IMPORTANT:** Be careful not to quit if your group is complete, because doing so will affect your earnings and may disqualify you from the task.

**Tips to Maximizing your Points**

- Because it has been five years since you all lived together, your memories of each other’s travel preferences may be outdated, so do not expect agreement to be easily reached.
- Even after making several proposals during the negotiation, you may still not reach an agreement that yields a positive score for everyone. So don't wait until the last minute to make proposals, make as many as needed.
- Listen carefully to what matters to the others while still advocating for what matters most to you as you make your official proposals.
roles.BATNA: Your best alternative to negotiated agreement ('BATNA') is to visit your parents, which is worth 0 points. If agreement is not reached, this is effectively what your outcome is worth.

Multiple_Afternoon_Activities.reason: Can feel overwhelming and not relaxing.

Plans_to_Meet_New_People.reason: Fun, and makes stories the group will remember.

Shared_Rooms.reason: The late-night chats and small moments matter.

Some_Solo_Activities.reason: For you, the trip is about spending time together.

Visits_to_Nearby_Towns.reason: Places like Tarragona and mountain villages sound exciting.

Hotel_Arrangements_in_the_City.reason: Being near the mountains keeps nearby towns and nature views close.

You’ve been talking for years about a visit to Barcelona, a culture-rich city that stays up late, with a mountain range on one side and the coast and long beaches on the other. But life is all about tradeoffs, right? You have to give a little to get a little, and you are confident that you can find a plan that works for everyone.

*As you prepare for the negotiation, you reflect on your general preferences for each topic under discussion and the two issues associated with each one, as summarized below.*

*Topic 1: Travel Pace.*
- [1] Slow mornings: Relaxation is your favorite part of a trip. You prefer slow mornings because you like easing into the day without a clock ruining your mood.
- [2] Multiple afternoon activities: And so, by the afternoon, you want breathing room. Too many activities turns the day into a checklist.

*Topic 2: Who We Connect With.*
- [3] Plans to meet new people: You can go either way on meeting new people. But if you don’t meet them, you’ll still be able to have a great weekend.
- [4] Other college friends join: You’re just as flexible on whether other college friends join. If you decide to include them, you can roll with it, and if not, you’re also happy keeping it small.

*Topic 3: Togetherness.*
- [5] Shared rooms: You’d rather have your own room. Having private space lets you recharge, so you can show up better for the group during the day.
- [6] Some solo activities: At the same time, you’d appreciate having some solo activities built in. Even a bit of time doing your own thing helps you reset and come back to the group feeling fresh.

*Topic 4: Trip Scope.*
- [7] Visits to nearby towns: You’d rather not leave the city for nearby towns. There’s just so much to see within Barcelona itself, and going outside feels taxing and not that exciting.
- [8] Hotel arrangements in the city: That’s why you want a hotel in or near the city. It lets you see more of the city, and anchors the group away from checking out nearby towns.

*Topic 5: Travel Experience.*
- [9] Long nature excursion: Given more time to fit into the schedule, you want to spend it on a longer nature excursion. Short hikes don’t do the Collserola mountain range, with its views over the Mediterranean coastline, justice.
- [10] More historical and cultural tours: And so, you just don’t want to spend more time touring the city. History is not your thing, adventure is.

**Your Prolific Bonus ($):**

- Your preferences are also captured in a points table shown below.
- Higher points mean you prefer that option more.
- If your group reaches an agreement, the **total points** from the chosen options determine the **bonus** payment you receive from Prolific.
- However, this must be an **official agreement** that everyone votes to accept in the system, which means it must have a **positive score for everyone**. Agreements made verbally will not count.
- If a player is **disconnected** or missing, then unfortunately this is outside of our control and your group cannot reach agreement. In that case, the **best option** is usually to press the **Quit** button and receive the base payment.
- **IMPORTANT:** Be careful not to quit if your group is complete, because doing so will affect your earnings and may disqualify you from the task.

**Tips to Maximizing your Points**

- Because it has been five years since you all lived together, your memories of each other’s travel preferences may be outdated, so do not expect agreement to be easily reached.
- Even after making several proposals during the negotiation, you may still not reach an agreement that yields a positive score for everyone. So don't wait until the last minute to make proposals, make as many as needed.
- Listen carefully to what matters to the others while still advocating for what matters most to you as you make your official proposals.

tips:
Negotiation is a skill you can get better at with practice. At its core, it is about making choices when people want different things, with each person’s decision affecting what the others get out of the deal. The good news is that a few basic ideas go a long way.

One of the most important ideas is knowing your best alternative to negotiating an agreement, or your BATNA. This is your realistic fallback plan outside the negotiation. Knowing what that alternative is actually worth helps protect you from accepting an agreement that leaves you worse off than walking away. A simple rule follows from this: never accept a deal worse than your BATNA.

Another key idea is that some negotiations can be win-win. This happens when people value different issues differently. If something matters a lot to you but not as much to the other person, you may be able to trade it for something you care about more. In practice, this means focusing less on “splitting the difference” and more on learning what the other person really wants, so you can make smart tradeoffs that leave both of you better off.

A final tip is that win-win outcomes become easier to achieve when everyone knows what matters most to each party . So try to be clear about what you care about, and how these interests translate to what you want across each issue under negotiation. At the same time, listen closely to what the other person cares about. Knowing this is what will make such smart, win-win tradeoffs possible.
